3249352.title: Why to use generics and generic programming?
3249352.transcript: In this chapter and in the coming lectures, we are going to talk about Java generics. So first of all, let's talk about the motivation behind generics. Okay. So generics are a facility of generic programming. These features were added to the Java programming language back in 2004. And of course, they were designed to extend Java's type system to allow a type or method to operate on objects of various types while providing compile time type safety. Okay. So first of all, let's talk about errors in domain in software engineering. Bugs are simply a fact of life, but some bugs are easier to detect than others. So there are two main types of bugs and errors, compile time errors and runtime errors. So let's talk about these two main types of errors. First of all, let's focus on compile time errors. It is easier to detect them as the application will not compile. So if we have a compile time error, it means that we are not able to run and execute our application. And of course this is why it is easier to detect them. The compilers error messages help to figure out what is the problem exactly. So compile time errors can be detected easily and can be fixed easily. So this is why we prefer compile time errors. On the other hand, what about runtime errors? Runtime errors are more problematic because it don't always surface immediately and it may be at a point in the program or the application that is far away from the actual cause of the problem. So this is why we don't like runtime errors. We prefer compile time errors and we will come to the conclusion that if we use generics then we can transform most of the errors into compile time errors. Okay. So let's talk about why to use generics. First of all, generics are the stability to your code. It makes most of the bugs and errors to be detectable at compile time and not runtime. And of course, we prefer compile time errors to runtime errors. Okay. So this is one of the crucial advantages of using generics. Then generics enable types such as classes and interfaces to be parameters when defining other classes, interfaces and methods. So we can reuse the same code with different inputs. So we are going to consider several examples. If we have a public void draw method that's waiting for a given parameter. In this case, our application is able to draw cars on the canvas. So in this case the car parameter is a value. But of course it is not a good approach because what if we would like to draw a bus instead of a car? What if we would like to draw, for example, a bicycle instead of a car? Then of course we may create multiple draw methods. The first draw method is going to draw cars. So this is why it is absolutely crucial that generics enable types such as classes and interfaces to be parameters when defining classes, interfaces and methods so we can reuse the same code with different inputs. Okay, so generics has many benefits over non generic code. First of all, stronger type checks at compile time. This is what we have been talking about. So if our code violates type safety, then the compiler warns us and this is exactly why we prefer compile time errors. Then the second advantage is that we can eliminate typecasting. What if we use generics? As you can see in this case, when using generics, we are dealing with the compiler. As far as the non generic implementation is concerned.
